clean_story: Earth’s silent tremors.  Feet, whiskers, and skin as antennas. "Detecting vibrations" means sensing tiny shakes in the air, ground, or water—like reading invisible ripples from a dropped pebble. We usually think of ears as the only vibration detectors, but animals use their entire bodies to "listen" to the world’s hidden pulses. A spider doesn’t need ears. Its eight legs are tuned like guitar strings, plucked by the faintest breeze or a fly’s footsteps on its web. Each hair on a leg bends with air currents, sending nerve signals that paint a 3D map of disturbances—no sound required. Elephants hear storms 100 miles away—not with their ears, but through their feet. Low-frequency rumbles travel faster through soil than air. When an elephant presses its toes down, bones and nerves act like a seismograph, translating ground waves into warnings or mating calls. Snakes lack outer ears, yet they detect vibrations via their jawbones. Resting their head on the ground turns their skull into a resonator—amplifying footsteps of prey like a stethoscope pressed to earth. Even fish use their swim bladders as internal microphones. The air-filled sac vibrates with underwater sounds, relaying pressure changes to their inner ear. Precision matters:A mole’s paw can distinguish between a worm wriggling 6 inches away and a raindrop hitting a leaf. Scorpions sense sand vibrations at 0. 00001 mm—smaller than a dust mote. Three related subjects are sensory evolution, bio-inspired tech and infra-sound hunting.
